summary: Jeff Atwood reflects on the tragic death of Aaron Swartz, expressing disappointment in himself for not supporting Aaron's defense, in the government for its prosecutorial overreach, and in the perverse effectiveness of martyrdom as a tool for change. He draws parallels between ragequitting in online communities and the finality of suicide, revealing his own struggles with similar thoughts. The post argues that activists must persist through injustice rather than give up, and that inflicting generational trauma on loved ones is unacceptable regardless of circumstances. A 2025 update commits to continuing Aaron's path of activism and love.
key_points: The government's prosecution of Aaron Swartz was grossly disproportionate and contributed directly to his death, yet the hacker community failed to rally adequately to his defense
Martyrdom as a strategy for change is perversely effective but ultimately wrong - Aaron would have achieved far more by prevailing and continuing his work
Suicide is the ultimate ragequit, ending not just the current game but all future games, and inflicts unacceptable generational trauma on those left behind
The path of the activist requires fighting through injustice, not succumbing to it, even when the system is corrupt and the odds seem insurmountable
Atwood candidly admits to researching suicide himself, positioning the post as advice from someone who understands the darkness firsthand
money_quotes: Aaron was to us hackers: an exemplar of the noble, selfless behavior and positive action that all hackers aspire to – but very few actually achieve.
I never knew Aaron, but I knew Aaron.
The idea that Aaron killing himself was a viable strategy, more than going on to prevail in this matter and so many more in his lifetime, makes me incredibly angry.
It is another thing entirely to play the final move and take your own life. To declare the end of this game and all future games, the end of ragequitting itself.
Inflicting generational trauma is absolutely unacceptable under any circumstances, no matter how dire.
The greater the injustice, the greater wrong undone when you ultimately prevail.
But do not, under any circumstances, give anyone the satisfaction of seeing you ragequit. They don't deserve it.
Play other, better moves – and consider your long game.
tone: raw, confessional, angry, grieving, ultimately defiant
key_insight: Suicide is the ultimate ragequit that denies yourself future victories and inflicts unbearable trauma on others - the activist's path demands endurance through injustice, not surrender to it.
